source: Rocky Linux 'About systemd' guide - unit file content ([Unit], [Service])

questions.q: In the [Unit] section, what do After= and Before= define?
options: The startup ORDER relative to other units
questions.why: After=/Before= set ordering: After=network.target means this unit starts after network.target.

questions.q: What is the difference between Requires= and Wants= in [Unit]?
options: With Requires=, if a dependency fails to activate the unit won't start; with Wants=, a failed dependency does not block it
Wants= stops the unit if the dependency fails
questions.why: Requires= is a hard dependency (failure blocks start); Wants= is soft (failure is tolerated).

options: If a listed unit is running, the current unit cannot run (and vice versa)
questions.why: Conflicts= is a negative dependency: the two units can't be active at the same time (e.g. postfix conflicts with sendmail).

questions.q: In [Service], what is the DEFAULT value of Type=?
options: simple - the service runs as the main process
questions.why: Type=simple is the default; forking is for daemons that fork, oneshot must exit before follow-ups, notify sends sd_notify.

questions.q: For a traditional daemon that forks into the background, which Type= should you use (often with PIDFile=)?
questions.why: Type=forking suits daemons that fork; PIDFile= then tells systemd the main PID.

options: Restarts the service only when it exits with a non-zero (unclean) status
Restarts only on a clean exit
questions.why: on-failure restarts on unclean exit/kill/timeout; 'no' (default) never restarts, 'always' restarts regardless, on-success only on clean exit.

questions.q: What does the default KillMode=control-group do when the unit stops?
options: Kills all remaining processes in the unit's control group
questions.why: control-group (default) kills every process in the unit's cgroup; 'process' kills only the main process, 'none' kills nothing.
